commblock.title: Testing Newgen Table 2 Commentary Regression 18-APR

row.entry: Confidentiality: All parties shall maintain strict confidentiality of proprietary information. testing
Confidentiality: Confidential information may be shared with affiliates under NDA.
Termination: This agreement may be terminated by either party with 30 days’ notice. sample
Termination: Termination requires a 60-day notice and mutual consen
Confidentiality: All parties shall maintain strict confidentiality of proprietary information. testing
123Confidentiality: Confidential information may be shared with affiliates under NDA.
Termination: This agreement may be terminated by either party with 30 days’ notice. sample
123Termination: Termination requires a 60-day notice and mutual consent.
General Clause: This clause applies equally to both contracts.
test

commblock.para: Table from Master sample 2 inserted below

row.entry: Less than 96 hours
72 hours
Less than 72 hours
48 hours
Less than 48 hours
24 hours
Less than 24 hours
12 hours

commblock.para: Table from Master sample 2 inserted below

row.entry: Algorithm (with no means of putting it into effect)
NOT patentable
Book
Fiction novel
Copyright
Box
Strawberry punnet box
Design

entry.caseref: Multisteps v Source and Sell (2013) AIPC ¶92-462; [2013] FCA 743

row.entry: Business method
NOT patentable
Colour
Cadbury purple: 1120615; Surf Life Saving red and yellow flag: 780743
Trade mark
Compilation/table
Copyright
Computer program
Copyright
Computer-related inventions
Computer processing apparatus for assembling text in Chinese language characters
May be patentable
DNA/genes
Isolated DNA; cDNA
NOT patentable
Idea
NOT patentable
Moving image
Toyota Jump: 1500012 Salt Bae: the act of putting salt onto meat: 1840549
Trade mark
non-English trade marks
“Volkswagen”: 118525 for Gong Cha tea: 1592767
Trade mark
Pharmaceutical drug
Patent
Scientific theory
NOT patentable
Script/screenplay for films and TV shows
Copyright
Shape
Coca Cola bottle: 172526; Toblerone chocolate: 2093276; Rubiks cube: 2312596
Trade mark
Smell/Scent
Eucalyptus Radiata scent for golf tees: 1241420; Cinammon scent for furniture: 1858042
Trade mark
Sound/jingle
Trade mark
The Act means the Copyright Act 1968 (Cth).
The adaptation right is one of the exclusive rights comprising the copyright in works, excluding artistic works, under ss 31(1)(iv) and 10(1) of the Act. Adaptation means:
in relation to a literary work in a non-dramatic form — a version of the work (whether in its original language or in a different language) in a dramatic form;
in relation to a literary work in a dramatic form — a version of the work (whether in its original language or in a different language) in a non-dramatic form;
(ba)

in relation to a literary work being a computer program — a version of the work (whether or not in the language, code or notation in which the work was originally expressed) not being a reproduction of the work;
in relation to a literary work (whether in a non-dramatic form or in a dramatic form):
a translation of the work; or
(ii)
a version of the work in which a story or action is conveyed solely or principally by means of pictures; and
in relation to a musical work — an arrangement or transcription of the work.
An artistic work under s 10(1) of the Act means
a painting, sculpture, drawing, engraving or photograph, whether the work is of artistic quality or not;
a building or a model of a building, whether the building or model is of artistic quality or not;
a work of artistic craftmanship that is not mentioned under (a) or (b) above.

A work of artistic craftmanship is not defined in the Act. It has been interpreted to mean a crafted item displaying a degree of manual skill on the part of the maker, which is judged to be of “artistic quality”.

The authorisation of an infringement of copyright in a work is a separate act of infringement under s 36(1) of the Act. In determining authorisation, the matters to be taken into account include, under s 36(1A) of the Act:
the extent (if any) of the person’s power to prevent the doing of the act concerned;
the nature of any relationship existing between the person and the person who did the act concerned; and
whether the person took any reasonable steps to prevent or avoid the doing of the act, including whether the person complied with any industry code.

The author is the human creator of an original work. In relation to photographs, s 10(1) of the Act provides that the author is the person who took the photograph. It is not otherwise defined in the Act. There is no authorship of sound recordings, cinematograph films, television broadcasts, sound broadcasts, and published editions of works.

A broadcast is a communication to the public delivered by a “broadcasting service” within the meaning of the Broadcasting Services Act 1992 (Cth). Under s 10(1) of the Act, a broadcast includes a television broadcast and a sound broadcast.
A cinematograph film is the aggregate of the visual images embodied in an article or thing so as to be capable by the use of that article or thing:
of being shown as a moving picture; or
of being embodied in another article or thing by the use of which it can be so shown;
and includes the aggregate of the sounds embodied in a sound-track associated with such visual images.

Communication to the public is one of the exclusive rights comprising the copyright in works under s 31(1) of the Act, and in sound recordings, cinematograph films, television broadcasts, sound broadcasts, and published editions of works, under ss 85–88 of the Act. To communicate, under s 10(1) of the Act, is to make copyright material available to the public online, or electronically transmit, whether over a path, or a combination of paths provided by a material substance or otherwise.

A computer program is a set of statements or instructions to be used directly or indirectly in a computer in order to bring about a certain result. Computer programs are literary works under s 10(1) of the Act.
Compulsory licences under Pt IVA the Act allow certain copyright materials to used, without infringement, but conditional upon the payment of “equitable remuneration” to the owner.
Under s 10(1) of the Act, a copy:
of a work — means a reproduction; or

of a sound recording — means a record embodying the sound recording, or a substantial part of the sound recording, derived directly or indirectly from a record produced on the making of the sound recording; or
of a cinematograph film — means any article or thing in which the visual images or sounds comprising the film are embodied; or
of a broadcast — includes:
a record embodying a sound recording of the whole or a part of the broadcast; or
(ii)
a copy of a cinematograph film of the whole or a part of the broadcast.

Copyright is the exclusive right of the owner to do, and authorise others to do, certain acts in relation to original works and subject matter other than works for a limited term. Copyright is intangible personal property under s 196(1) of the Act. Copyright only subsists under the Act.

Copying is to make a copy of an original work. Copying provides the element of causation in the infringing reproduction of works. The original work must be the source from which the infringing work is derived, although it need not be the direct source.

In the absence of copying, the independent creation of a new work does not infringe copyright in a subsisting original work. A separate copyright subsists in the independently created work, as a separate original work in its own right.

In Pt IV of the Act, the exclusive right to “make a copy” comprises part of the copyright in sound recordings, cinematograph films, television broadcasts, sound broadcasts, and published editions of works, under ss 85–88 of the Act.
Copyright material is anything in which copyright subsists under the Act.

Crown copyright is the copyright subsisting in works and subject matter other than works which are made “by or at the direction” of the Commonwealth, or a State or Territory of Australia under Pt VII of the Act.
A dramatic work is a work or an adaptation of a work to be performed or represented, rather than narrated. A dramatic work includes
a choreographic show or other dumb show; and
a scenario or script for a film; under s 10(1) of the Act.

An exclusive licence of copyright, as defined in s 10(1) of the Act, means a licence in writing, signed by or on behalf of the owner of copyright, authorising the licensee, to the exclusion of all other persons, to do an act that the owner of the copyright would, but for the licence, have the exclusive right to do.
Copyright, in literary, dramatic, musical and artistic works, comprises the following exclusive rights of the owner, under s 31(1) of the Act:
to reproduce the work in a material form;
(ii)
to publish the work;
(iii)
to perform the work in public, (excluding artistic works);
(iv)
to communicate the work to the public;
to make an adaptation of the work (excluding artistic works).

In the case of a literary work (other than a computer program) or a musical or dramatic work, to enter into a commercial rental arrangement in respect of the work reproduced in a sound recording. In the case of a computer program, to enter into a commercial rental arrangement in respect of the program.

entry.emphasis: Exclusive rights in subject matter other than works

row.entry: Copyright in subject matter other than works comprises the following exclusive rights of the owner:
to make a copy of the sound recording: s 85(1)(a); or
(ii)
to cause the recording to be heard in public: s 85(1)(b); or
(iii)
to communicate the recording to be heard in public: s 85(1)(c); or
(iv)
to enter into a commercial rental arrangement: s 85(1)(d).
to make a copy of the film: s 86(a); or
(ii)
to cause the film in so far as consists of images, to be seen in public or, in so far as it consists of sound, to be heard in public: s 86(b); or
(iii)
to communicate the film to the public: s 86(c).
to make a copy of the television broadcast or sound broadcast: s 87(a) and (b).
(ii)
to re-broadcast or otherwise communicate to the public a television broadcast or a sound broadcast: s 87(c)
to make a “facsimile copy” of the published edition: s 88.

A fair dealing is not defined in the Act. It is the basis for a series of statutory defences or exceptions to infringement of copyright in works, according to the purpose of the fair dealing. There is no general defence of fair dealing. The Act only allows fair dealings for the purposes of:
research or study (s 40);
(ii)
criticism or review (s 41);
(iii)
parody or satire (s 41A);
(iv)
reporting news (s 42);
judicial proceedings or professional advice (s 43); and
(vi)
access by persons with a disability (s 113E).
Copyright protection only applies to the form in which ideas are expressed, not to the ideas themselves.

Under s 36(1) of the Act, the copyright in works is directly infringed by a person who, not being the owner of the copyright, and without the licence of the copyright owner, does in Australia, or authorises the doing in Australia of, any act comprised in the copyright in the work.
Indirect infringement of copyright refers to acts done in relation to infringing goods or articles without the licence of the copyright owner. These acts of indirect infringement are:
importation into Australia for sale or hire (s 102); and
sold, hired out, or otherwise made the subject of trade in Australia (s 103)

A work of joint authorship means a work that has been produced by the collaboration of 2 or more authors in which the contribution of each author is not separate from the contribution of the other author or the contributions of the other authors, under s 10(1) of the Act.
A licence of copyright is a permission from the owner to use copyright material in a way that would otherwise be an infringement of the copyright. See Exclusive Licensee

Literary works are not exhaustively defined in the Act. Literary works are any combination of words, numbers or symbols that express meaning or information. No literary merit is required. Examples of literary works include: books, novels, poems, song lyrics, letters, newspaper articles, brochures, advertising catalogues, telephone directories, and blank business, accounting and betting forms. Under s 10(1) of the Act, literary works include (a) tables and compilations expressed in words, numbers or symbols, and (b) computer programs or compilations of computer programs.
Under s 29A(1) of the Act, a work is made public when:
the work, or an adaptation of the work, is:
published, performed in public, broadcast or otherwise communicated to the public; or
(ii)
if the work is an artistic work — exhibited in public; or
if the work is an artistic work included in a cinematograph film — the film is seen in public; or
if the work is a building — the building has been constructed; or
records of the work, or of an adaptation of the work, are:
offered to the public (whether or not for sale); or
(ii)
exposed for sale to the public.
Copyright material other than a work is made public when:
the work, or an adaptation of the work, is:
published, performed in public, broadcast or otherwise communicated to the public; or
(ii)
if the work is an artistic work — exhibited in public; or
if the work is an artistic work included in a cinematograph film — the film is seen in public; or
if the work is a building — the building has been constructed; or
records of the work, or of an adaptation of the work, are:
offered to the public (whether or not for sale); or
(ii)
exposed for sale to the public.
Under s 29A(2) of the Act, other copyright material is made public when:
it is published; or
if the material is a sound recording — it is:
heard in public; or
(ii)
communicated to the public; or
if the material is a cinematograph film — it is:
seen in public (to the extent it consists of visual images); or
(ii)
heard in public (to the extent it consists of sounds); or
(iii)
communicated to the public; or
copies of the material are:
offered to the public (whether or not for sale); or
(ii)
exposed for sale to the public.

Material form, in relation to a work or an adaptation of a work, includes under s 10(1) of the Act, any form (whether visible or not) of storage of the work or adaptation, or a substantial part of the work or adaptation, whether or not the work or adaptation, or a substantial part of the work or adaptation, can be reproduced.
Moral rights of authors comprise 3 rights in relation to original works:
the author’s right of attribution of authorship of works: s 193(1);
(ii)
the author’s right not to have authorship falsely attributed: s 195AC(1); and
(iii)
the author’s right of integrity of authorship of works: s 195A1(1).
Moral rights of performers are set out in Pt IX of the Act, which comprise 3 similar rights of attribution of performership, no false attribution of performership, and integrity of performership.

Musical works are no longer defined in the Act. Musical works include songs, tunes, orchestral works and musicals, once the work is fixed in material form. Melody or harmony are no longer required under the Act. Song lyrics are separate literary works.
Original works are not defined in the Act. Original works are works that are not copied from other works.
Orphan works are works where the author is not generally known.
The general rule is that the author is the owner of copyright in works under s 35(2) of the Act. The exceptions are:
works produced by an employee in the course of employment: s 35(6);
(ii)
the work of journalists: s 35(4); and
(iii)
commissions for making certain artistic works: s 35(5)
These rules can be excluded or modified by contract: s 35(3).
The maker of a sound recording, cinematograph film, television or sound broadcast is the owner of copyright under ss 97(2), 98(2), 99 and 100 of the Act.
To perform in public is one of the exclusive rights comprising the copyright in works, under s 32(1) of the Act. Under s 27(1) of the Act, a reference in the Act to performance shall —

be read as including a reference to any mode of visual or aural presentation, whether the presentation is by the use of reception equipment, by the exhibition of a cinematograph film, by the use of a record or by any other means; and
in relation to a lecture, address, speech or sermon — be read as including a reference to delivery.

Whether the performance is in public is not defined in the Act. If the performance is an adjunct to a commercial activity, it is likely to be in public. This is contrasted with a “private and domestic” audience.
Certain performances at premises where people reside or sleep are not in public under s 46 of the Act.

Performers’ protection is a separate regime of rights of performers under Div XIA of the Act. Performers’ protection is protection against the “unauthorised use” of performances, without the consent of the performer. Under s 248G of the Act, unauthorised uses are
making a direct or indirect recording of the performance; or
communicating the performance to the public, either directly from the live performance or from an unauthorised recording of it.
There is no copyright in performances. Performers’ rights under Div XIA of the Act are separate and distinct from the moral rights of performers.

To publish a work is one of the exclusive rights comprising copyright in works. Under s 29(1)(a) of the Act, a work is only published if reproductions of it have been supplied to the public, whether by sale or otherwise.
To reproduce in material form is one of the exclusive rights comprising copyright in works. Infringement of copyright in works by reproduction in material form involves 2 elements:
objective similarity between the original work, or a substantial part of the original work, and the infringing work; and
(ii)
causal connection between the original work, or a substantial part of the original work, and the infringing work by copying.
The following acts are deemed by s 21 of the Act to constitute reproduction in material form:
making a sound recording or cinematograph film of a work: s 21(1);
(ii)
embodying a work into an article by converting the work into or from a digital or other electronic or machine-readable form: s 21(1A);
(iii)
producing in a 3-dimensional form a version of a 2-dimensional artistic work: s 21(3)(a);
(iv)
producing in a 2-dimensional form a version of a 3-dimensional artistic work: s 21(3)(b);
producing an object code version from a source code version of a computer program, and vice versa: s 21(5); and
(vi)
converting a sound recording or cinematograph film into or out of a digital or other electronic machine-readable form: s 21(6).
Under s 10(1) of the Act, a sound recording means the aggregate of the sounds embodied in a record.
Under s 10(1) of the Act, a sound broadcast means sounds broadcast otherwise than as part of a television broadcast. It refers to radio broadcasts.

The subject matter of copyright other than works are sound recordings, cinematograph films, television broadcasts, sound broadcasts, and published editions of works, under ss 85, 86, 87 and 88 of the Act.

Under s 14(1)(b) of the Act, a reproduction, adaptation or copy of a work includes a substantial part of the work, if not the whole work. A substantial part is qualitative, not necessarily quantitative.
Under s 10(1) of the Act, a television broadcast means visual images broadcast by way of television, together with any sounds broadcast for reception along with those images.

Under s 33 of the Act, the term of copyright in works is generally the lifetime of the author and 70 years after. The term of copyright in sound recordings and cinematograph films is generally 70 years after being made public. The term of copyright in broadcasts is generally 50 years after the broadcast was made. For published editions the term is 25 years after publication of the edition.
Works are literary, dramatic, musical and artistic works.

commblock.para: ATAG 1 - Client impact statement: Refreshed tax guidance released on short-term rental income and deductions

row.entry: Deductible portion
Days rented (or genuinely available for rent)
Total days in year

commblock.para: ATAG 2 - Client impact statement: Refreshed tax guidance released on short-term rental income and deductions

row.entry: Deductible portion
Typically, this will arise from a phone call from the client (or prospective client) where they ask:
&box;
&box;
“You’ve recommended our new Company be an LTC (or stay a QC). Why is this?”
&box;
&box;

entry.emphasis: Action to take because of the enquiry

row.entry: &box;
&box;
Personalise the fact sheet as much as possible. Update the conclusion specifically for the client.
&box;
&box;
Consider adding a general disclaimer if the advice is very general in nature. Encourage the client to come and see you for more specific advice if needed.
&box;
&box;
Have the fact sheet reviewed at the appropriate level before sending.
&box;
&box;
If the client comes back to you for further advice, refer to the guides:
&box;
&box;
Inland Revenue guide IR435 on QCs
Inland Revenue guide IR879 on LTCs
Typically this will arise from a phone call from the client (or prospective client) where they ask:
“I’ve heard about the Penny & Hooper decision and I don’t know whether it changes things for the way our business is structured. Can you advise me please?”
“I’m thinking of restructuring my business. Can you advise me?”

entry.emphasis: Action to take as a result of the enquiry

row.entry: &box;
&box;
Personalise the fact sheet as much as possible. Remember to update the conclusion specifically for the client.
&box;
&box;
Consider adding a general disclaimer if the advice is very general in nature. Encourage the client to come and see you for more specific advice if needed.
&box;
&box;
Have the fact sheet reviewed at the appropriate level before sending.
&box;
&box;
&box;
&box;

commblock.para: NPTH - 23-210 Guide: Have funds been lent at favourable interest rates to a person associated with an employee?

row.entry: Two companies
Where a group of people together own 50% or more of the shares in both companies.
Russell and his wife Janine hold 50% each of the shares in Co A. They also hold 50% each of the shares in Co B.
Where a group of people control both companies by any other means.
Co A and Co B are associated because a group of people (Russell and Janine) together own 50% or more of the shares in both companies.
A company and a person other than a company
Where the person holds at least 25% of the shares in the company.
Rose holds 30% of the shares in Co X. Rose is associated to Co X because she holds at least 25% of the shares in Co X.
Two relatives
If they are within 2 degrees of blood relationship.
1. Matthew and Anna are siblings. They are associated to each other as they are within 2 degrees of blood relationship to each other.
If they are married, in a civil union or in a de facto relationship.
2. Mr and Mrs Davies are associated to each other as they are married.
If one person is within 2 degrees of blood relationship to the other person's spouse, civil union partner or de facto partner.
3. Denise is Mr Davies' sister. Denise is associated to Mrs Davies as she is within 2 degrees of blood relationship to Mr Davies.
Person and a trust for the person's relative
A person (“X”) and a trust under which X's associated relative (see row above) is a beneficiary.

Rona is a beneficiary of a trust. Rona has a sister Marge. Marge is associated to the trust because her sister (within 2 degrees of blood relationship) is a beneficiary of the trust. This is despite the fact that Marge is not a beneficiary of the trust.
Trust and beneficiary
A trust and a beneficiary of that trust are associated.
In the example directly above, Rona is associated to the trust because she is a beneficiary.
Two trusts with common settlor
Two trusts are associated where each trust has a common settlor.
Ronald settles two trusts, Trust X and Trust Y. The two trusts are associated to each other because they have a common settlor, Ronald.
Trust and settlor
A trust and the settlor of the trust.
In the example directly above, Ronald will be associated to Trust X and Trust Y because he has settled each trust.
Settlor and beneficiary
A settlor of a trust and the beneficiaries of that same trust.

Nelson is the settlor of the Nelson Trust. One of the beneficiaries of the Nelson Trust is Nelson's best friend, Marty. Nelson will be associated to Marty because Marty is a beneficiary of a trust that Nelson has settled.
Partnership and partner
A partnership and a partner of the partnership are associated. Note other rules apply to limited partnerships.
Ruth is a partner in a partnership. Ruth is associated to the partnership.

commblock.para: NPTH - 361-010 Fact sheet: Working for families

row.entry: Attributable fringe benefits

Jack and Diane are married with three children. Together they have a 100% voting interest in a plumbing company and are both employed by the business. They each receive a salary of $35,000. The company provides each of them with a work vehicle which they can also use for private use. The value of the private use of the vehicle plus the FBT payable by the company is $8,000 for Jack and $6,000 for Diane for the tax year.
Jack has a student loan and he and Diane receive WfFTC.
Jack will need to include $8,000 for the calculation of WfFTC and his student loan.
Diane will need to include $6,000 as income for the calculation of her WfFTC entitlement.
Non-locked-in PIE income

George receives an inheritance of $400,000 and invests it in a cash PIE that is not a superannuation fund or retirement savings scheme. He receives an annual income of $20,000 on that investment. George has used the correct prescribed investor rate (PIR) so he has not included the PIE income in his IR3.
George will have to include the PIE income of $20,000 as part of his income for WfFTC and student loans.
Passive income of children

Curtis is a child in the care of his mother Kim. Kim receives WfFTC for him. Curtis is gifted $15,000 from his grandmother in April. This money is invested in term deposits. At the end of the tax year Curtis receives a letter from the bank showing he earned $600 interest. As the total interest earned by him is over $500, Kim will need to include the amount over $500 (i.e., $100) as part of the family income for WfFTC purposes.
Income of non-resident spouse or partner

Christine lives in New Zealand with her children and receives WfFTC. Her de facto partner Al works overseas. Al is not a tax resident in New Zealand. For the tax year he receives an income which is equal to NZ $80,000.
Christine will need to include Al’s income, of NZ $80,000 as family income for WfFTC.
Tax exempt salary or wages
Joseph is a New Zealand tax resident and works for the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD).
Although Joseph’s income is exempt from income tax, he will need to include his salary as part of his income for WfFTC and student loans.
Pensions and annuities
Before David retired, he had been paying into a private superannuation plan run by his employer. Now that he has retired he receives weekly pension payments which total $20,000 each year.
David will have to include 50% of the annual value of these payments ($10,000) as part of his income for WfFTC and student loans.
Other income
Olivia’s parents give her $100 a week which she puts towards the mortgage payments. The total amount she received over the year is $5,200.
Olivia will need to include the total amount of $5,200 as part of her income for WfFTC purposes because it is more than $5,000.
Income equalisation scheme deposits

Sarah and Kevin receive WfFTC payments for their two children and Kevin has a student loan. Kevin makes a deposit of $50,000 into the income equalisation scheme on 1 June 2023. He can claim a deduction in his income tax return for the period 1 April 2023 to 31 March 2024 of the amount of the deposit. For the 2024 tax year Kevin earns $80,000.
His deduction from the income equalisation deposit of $50,000 makes his taxable income $30,000. Kevin is required to add back into his income the $50,000 for WfFTC and student loans.
Salary exchanged for private use of an employer-provided motor vehicle
When Erin started work, her employer offered her a:
• $50,000 salary and the private use of the work car, or
• $58,000 salary without private use of the work car
Erin decided to use the employer-provided car instead of the $8,000 in additional salary. Erin must include the $8,000 as income for WfFTC and student loans.
Vouchers and other short-term charge facilities

George is employed by a local clothes shop as a store assistant. The shop often pays their staff with grocery vouchers. In addition to his pay of $200 George is given a $25 grocery voucher each week. This gives George an annual wage of $10,400 and vouchers worth $1,300.

The threshold that applies to George is 5% of his wages, which is $520 ($10,400 × 5%). As the value of the vouchers is more than $520, George will need to include the value ($1,300) plus any FBT his employer has paid on the vouchers, in his income for WfFTC and student loans.
Distributions from retirement savings schemes

Te Rūnanga o Ngāi Tahu makes contributions to John’s retirement savings scheme account of $1,200 (before deducting RSCT) for the year. Te Rūnanga makes a distribution to John of $1,200. John has not yet retired or become eligible for NZ Super.
John needs to include the $1,200 as part of his income for WfFTC and student loans.
Distributions from superannuation schemes

Over the year Denise’s employer contributes $10,000 to Denise’s superannuation scheme. Denise herself contributes $15,000 to the scheme. In that same year, Denise receives a distribution from her scheme of the $25,000 that she and her employer contributed in that year, which earned $250 in interest. Denise still works for the same employer.

Denise needs to include the $10,000 contributed by her employer and the $250 in interest as part of her income for WfFTC and student loans. She doesn’t need to include the $15,000 which she had contributed toward the distribution.
Tax-exempt overseas pensions

Cory receives a $10,000 UK pension that is like NZ Super. He doesn’t pay his UK pension to Work and Income. The full entitlement to NZ Super is $14,000 (example only). He receives $4,000 NZ Super less tax and keeps his $10,000 UK pension. To ensure Cory receives the same net amount as a person on full NZ Super, Cory’s UK pension is tax exempt.
Cory needs to include the $10,000 overseas pension as part of his income for WfFTC and student loans. The $4,000 he receives in NZ Super will be automatically included.
Losses

Frank has self-employed income from two unrelated businesses, a plastering business, and a shoe shop. The plastering business made a profit of $30,000, while the shoe shop made a loss of $10,000. Frank puts the profit of $20,000 in the self-employed income box in his IR3.

entry.emphasis: Note that losses from other businesses/investments/rental properties no longer reduce income for the purposes of working out the Working for Families tax credits entitlement.

row.entry: Frank’s student loan repayment obligation and WfFTC entitlement will be based on $30,000 of income from the plastering business.
Major shareholder in a close company attributed income

Jasmine is receiving WfFTC for her two children and holds 60,000 of the 100,000 voting interests in the company. Her two children hold the other 40,000. The company made a profit of $90,000 and didn’t pay any dividends.
Jasmine’s attributed income calculation for her shares
Jasmine’s attributed income calculation for her dependent children’s shares
Distributions from a trust that are not beneficiary income

Matt is the beneficiary of a family trust that has a cash PIE investment. The income was taxed at the 28% notified prescribed investor rate (PIR). This means that when the trust pays this income on to Matt, he does not need to include it in his tax return.
The trust does not have any other taxable income so any distributions it makes to Matt are not treated as beneficiary income.

The trust distributes $2,000 income from the PIE investment to Matt. He does not include the income in his tax return, but the distribution is part of his income for Working for Families and student loans and he needs to let Inland Revenue know how much he received.
Non-resident borrower’s overseas income

Sarah has a $50,000 student loan and is working overseas under a voluntary service abroad approved program with a salary of $27,000 (in NZ dollars) for the year. Inland Revenue have approved her application to be treated as physically present in New Zealand.
Sarah has no other income so will only need to tell Inland Revenue about her overseas salary of $27,000.
Inland Revenue will apply the student loan repayment threshold of $22,828 (applicable for the 2024 income tax year) when assessing her student loan repayment.
Settlor’s attributable trustee income (for 2015 tax year onwards)
Gavin and Stacey receive Working for Families payments for their 3 children and Gavin has a student loan.
They are also the only settlors of the “A Family Trust” trust. The trust has a voting interest of 67% in a building company. Both Gavin and Stacey work for the company.
The building company has net income of $200,000 and paid dividends of $40,000. The trust also received $4,500 interest that was treated as trustee income.
Trustee income is $4,500
Company income is:
Attributed trustee income is $55,850.

Gavin and Stacey will have to include attributable trustee income of a total of $111,700 ($55,850 each) as part of their family income for Working for Families Tax Credits. Gavin’s $55,850 will also be included as income for calculating his student loan repayment obligation.
